mnemonics.title: Balancing Scale
mnemonics.content: Class balancing is like a scale: add weight to the minority class (oversampling) or remove weight from the majority class (undersampling) to achieve equilibrium.

mnemonics.title: Outlier Isolation
mnemonics.content: Outlier detection isolates anomalies: think of it as finding a unique person in a crowd by quickly isolating those who stand out, like in Isolation Forest.

mnemonics.title: SMOTE Synthesis
mnemonics.content: SMOTE creates synthetic minority samples by interpolating between existing minority points, filling the gap like connecting dots to form new points.

acronyms.usage: Used to generate synthetic samples for the minority class to balance the dataset by interpolating between existing minority instances.
A density-based outlier detection method that compares the local density of a point to its neighbors; points with significantly lower density are outliers.
A statistical measure used to detect outliers; points falling below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR are considered outliers.

acronyms.expansion: Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under Curve
acronyms.usage: An evaluation metric for classification models on imbalanced datasets; a higher AUC indicates better discrimination between classes.

visualTips.visualization: Visualize a bar chart where one bar (majority class) is very tall, like a skyscraper, while other bars (minority classes) are short, like small houses. This imbalance needs balancing.
Imagine a scatter plot where most points form a dense cluster (normal data), but a few points are scattered far away (outliers), like islands distant from the mainland.
Think of minority class points as dots on a graph; SMOTE draws lines between nearest minority points and places new points along these lines, like filling gaps between stars in a constellation.

analogies.analogy: It's like a classroom with 30 students from Grade A and 5 from Grade B; to balance, you either invite more Grade B students (oversampling) or ask some Grade A students to leave (undersampling).
Detecting outliers is like finding a counterfeit bill among genuine ones; the counterfeit is rare and different, similar to an outlier in a dataset.

Think of Isolation Forest as a game of 'twenty questions' to isolate outliers: randomly select features and split values; outliers are isolated quickly because they differ significantly, like finding a unique item in a pile with few questions.
